ontology.foodex2_description: Meat dish, mutton/lamb based, cooked with spices
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Rara Gosht. A0CKP (cooked meat dish) used as the closest defensible analogous anchor for a spiced mutton preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Rara Gosht or a sufficiently close mutton curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mutton Rara (Rara Gosht) is approximately 300 g, comprising bone-in mutton pieces and minced mutton in a rich spiced gravy, usually served with naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton curry, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Rara Gosht is not listed individually in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry (cooked, bone-in, with spices and fat) as the closest approved analogous entry. Rara Gosht uses a dual-cut technique (pieces + mince) which slightly elevates protein and fat compared to a standard mutton curry; values are adjusted conservatively upward by approximately 5–8% to reflect the higher meat density. Energy cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked lamb/mutton curry (FDC ID 2103689).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of meat-based curry dishes — analogous estimate based on lamb/mutton curry data; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Rara Gosht. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous meat-based curry dishes with minimal carbohydrate content. Meat-dominant dishes with spiced gravy and low starch content consistently fall in the GI 20–30 range per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (25 × 3.8 × 3) / 100 = 2.85, rounded to 2.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less and request reduced-salt preparation, as a standard 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.8 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~25)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rara Gosht has a low glycemic index of approximately 25 and a glycemic load of under 3 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~5.2 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~62 mg per 100 g) from mutton and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Due to moderate saturated fat and cholesterol content from mutton and ghee, individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (150 g or less) and prefer preparations using vegetable oil rather than ghee to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Rara, also known as Rara Gosht, is a rich and intensely flavoured North Indian mutton dish distinguished by its unique dual-cut technique: it combines bone-in mutton pieces with minced mutton (keema) cooked together in a single pot. This technique creates a thick, deeply spiced gravy that is simultaneously meaty in texture and silky from the rendered fat of the mince. The dish originates from the Mughal-influenced culinary traditions of North India, particularly associated with Punjab and Uttar Pradesh. It is prepared by first browning onions in ghee or oil, then building a masala base with ginger, garlic, tomatoes, yoghurt, and whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, red chilli, turmeric, and garam masala. The mutton pieces and mince are added and slow-cooked until the meat is tender and the gravy has reduced to a thick, clinging consistency. The flavour profile is bold, spicy, and umami-rich with a slight tang from yoghurt and tomato. Nutritionally, Rara Gosht is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with very low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. It provides approximately 14.5 g protein and 13 g fat per 100 g. The sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to salt and spice blends, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is low (~25), making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served without high-GI accompaniments. In Singapore, Rara Gosht is served at North Indian restaurants such as Mirch Masalah, Khushboo, and Curry Village, typically priced around SGD 21.90 per portion and served with naan or basmati rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Rara Gosht?
Is Rara Gosht good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Rara Gosht and regular mutton curry?
Is Rara Gosht high in protein?
Where can I eat Rara Gosht in Singapore?
What spices are in Rara Gosht?

confidence_reason: Rara Gosht is not listed as a standalone entry in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry (cooked) with conservative upward adjustment to reflect the higher meat density of the dual-cut preparation. GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable meat-based curry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry (cooked) as no standalone Rara Gosht entry exists in any approved primary source. A conservative 5–8% upward adjustment was applied to protein and fat to reflect the dual-cut (pieces + mince) preparation method. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN mutton curry source entry. 3. vitamin_e_mg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN mutton curry source entry. 4. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN mutton curry source entry. 5. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Rara Gosht was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 6. The input food_name 'Rara Ghost' is a phonetic misspelling of 'Rara Gosht'; the canonical English name has been corrected to 'Mutton Rara (Rara Gosht)'. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the dish being a standard North Indian Muslim-origin preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified independently. 8. Gluten-free status assumes no wheat-based thickeners are used; restaurant preparations may vary.
